---
title: "RPP SRAM API Introduction"
subtitle: "rpp_smgr.h Interface Guide · Demo Session Version"
author: "XDL Technical Support"
date: "2026-03-17"
lang: en

format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [league, xdl-tech-theme.scss]
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        <link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.googleapis.com">
        <link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.gstatic.com" crossorigin>
        <link href="https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=IBM+Plex+Mono:wght@400;500;600&family=IBM+Plex+Sans:wght@400;500;600;700&display=swap" rel="stylesheet">
    slide-number: true
    chalkboard: false
    preview-links: auto
    small-footer: "XDL RPP"
    transition: slide
    background-transition: fade
    width: 1280
    height: 720
  pptx:
    theme: simple
    slide-number: true
    footer: "XDL RPP"
execute:
  echo: false
code-fold: false
logo: pic/logo_color_horizontal.png
reference-doc: template_xdl3.pptx
---

## Scope and Goal

- Goal: quickly understand lifecycle, allocation, transfer, and reformat APIs in `rpp_smgr.h`
- Scope: RPP managed SRAM mode development, training, and on-site debugging
- Audience: developers who already know basic RPP runtime and kernel launch flow



## RPP SRAM API Overview

- Header: `rpp_smgr.h`
- Namespace: `rppsmgr`
- Responsibility: manage mapping, transfer, and format conversion between device DDR and on-chip SRAM
- Access pattern: singleton manager

```cpp
rppsmgr::SRamManager& smgr = rppsmgr::SRamManager::GetInstance();
```



## Lifecycle APIs

| API | Purpose | Typical Usage Time |
|---|---|---|
| `Init(workspace_size, is_profile=false)` | Create manager-controlled SRAM workspace | Before entering managed SRAM flow |
| `Destroy()` | Release workspace and reset manager state | After managed SRAM flow (before program exit) |
| `Clear()` | Clear allocation records, keep manager instance alive | Reuse manager across multiple cases in one process |
| `IsInitialized()` / `GetWorkspaceSize()` | Query status | Debug/assert checks |
| `IsProfile()` / `SetProfile(bool)` | Profiling mode switch | Skip `Dump` in profile mode, etc. |

Note: call `Destroy()` explicitly before Driver teardown to avoid static destruction order issues.



## Init and Destroy

```cpp
Result Init(uint32_t workspace_size, bool is_profile = false);
void Destroy();
```

- `workspace_size`: SRAM workspace size in bytes, upper bound around 24 MB
- Common usage: `smgr.Init(SRAM_WORKSPACE)` (`SRAM_WORKSPACE` is defined in `rpp_block_segment.h`)
- `Destroy()` releases SRAM and internal SRAM I/O resources in the teardown stage



## Allocation: `PitchAllocate` / `Allocate`

`PitchAllocate` (explicit pitch):

```cpp
Result PitchAllocate(void* ddr_address, uint32_t height, uint32_t pitch_size,
                     ElementSize element_size, RppFormat_e format);
```

- Best for pitched 2D buffers where row stride is already known (image/matrix/tensor)

`Allocate` (auto pitch = `(width+31)/32*32 * element_size`):

```cpp
Result Allocate(void* ddr_address, uint32_t height, uint32_t width,
                ElementSize element_size, RppFormat_e format);
```

- Best for simpler cases where only logical width is known
- `ElementSize`: `E_SIZE_1` / `E_SIZE_2` / `E_SIZE_4` / `E_SIZE_8`



## Allocation: Tensor Helpers

```cpp
Result AllocateTensor1D<T>(void* ddr_address, uint32_t width, rppTensor1D<T>* rpp_tensor);
Result AllocateTensor2D<T>(void* ddr_address, uint32_t height, uint32_t width, rppTensor2D<T>* rpp_tensor);
Result AllocateTensor3D<T>(void* ddr_address, uint32_t dim2, uint32_t dim1, uint32_t dim0, rppTensor3D<T>* rpp_tensor);
```

- Best for tensor-style kernels using `get()` / `get_cont()` / `get_algn()` / `set_*()`
- Completes both Allocate and tensor metadata initialization
- `rpp_tensor` points to SRAM storage
- Reference: `ut_tensor/ut_tensor.cu`



## Free and Query

Free APIs: `Free(ddr_address)` / `FreeTensor(tensor)`

Query APIs:

```cpp
void* GetSRamAddr(void* ddr_address);     // kernel-facing SRAM address
Result GetSRamFormat(void* ddr_address, RppFormat_e& format);
```

- `GetSRamAddr`: key entry for kernel access under managed SRAM mode
- `GetSRamFormat`: useful for layout switching and format-aware pipeline debugging



## Data Transfer: Download / Upload

- DDR -> SRAM: `Download` / `Download2d` / `DownloadTensor`
- SRAM -> DDR: `Upload` / `Upload2d` / `UploadTensor`

- `Download/Upload`: managed D2S/S2D wrappers (internally call `rppMemcpyDtoSAsync` / `rppMemcpyStoDAsync`)
- `Download2d/Upload2d`: for 2D copy when device pitch and SRAM pitch differ
- `DownloadTensor/UploadTensor`: tensor path with automatic format-triggered Reformat



## What Is Reformat?

Problem: different operators may require different memory layouts (for example row-major vs column-major, interleaved vs split complex layout).

Reformat means rearranging data layout in memory without changing the data meaning.

Common format pairs: `HFMT <-> RFMT`, `HCFMT <-> RCFMT` (often used in FFT workflows).



## HFMT / RFMT and HCFMT / RCFMT

A practical way to read these pairs is: host-friendly layout vs RPP-compute-friendly layout.

- `HFMT`: host-friendly format, usually the layout that is easier to consume on host side
- `RFMT`: RPP-friendly format, usually the layout preferred by RPP operators for compute access
- `HCFMT`: host-friendly complex-data format
- `RCFMT`: RPP-friendly complex-data format

Two common conversion patterns in real pipelines:

1. Input is prepared as `HFMT` (or `HCFMT`) in host/DDR, then converted to `RFMT` (or `RCFMT`) before compute
2. After compute, data is converted back to `HFMT` (or `HCFMT`) for host-side validation, post-process, or export

Practical recommendations:

- Prefer `DownloadTensor` / `UploadTensor` so manager triggers Reformat automatically from format metadata
- If you manually call Reformat APIs, verify current format with `GetSRamFormat(...)` before the next compute stage



## Reformat API Comparison (Where Conversion Happens)

| Conversion Location | API | One-line Summary |
|---|---|---|
| In SRAM, same buffer | `ReformatInPlace` | In-place conversion, saves space; width must be < 4096 |
| In SRAM, src -> dst buffers | `ReformatTo` | Convert from source SRAM buffer to destination SRAM buffer |
| Through CPU/DDR fallback | `ReformatOutPlace` | Used when SRAM cannot hold temporary shape (large width) |
| After upload, on DDR | `ReformatDDR` | Convert in DDR for host-side or next-stage consumption |



## Reformat Selection Guide

Most cases: use `DownloadTensor` / `UploadTensor`; manager automatically triggers the required Reformat by format metadata.

Manual selection cases:

1. Operator needs another SRAM format: `ReformatInPlace` (small shape) or `ReformatOutPlace` (large shape)
2. Source and target are different buffers: `ReformatTo`
3. Data already uploaded, DDR still needs specific format: `ReformatDDR`



## Reserved Space Management

| API | Description | Typical Usage |
|---|---|---|
| `ReserveAllocate(size, p_base)` | Reserve SRAM space from workspace tail | Scratch SRAM for algorithms without new mapping objects |
| `FetchReservedSpace(p_base)` | Fetch reserved space (Upload first if DDR-bound) | Use reserved area in current stage |
| `ReleaseReservedSpace()` | Release reservation (Download first if DDR-bound) | End of current stage |
| `ReserveFree()` | Cancel reservation and return unused region | Reservation no longer needed |

- Typical use: reduction, tree-reduction buffers, multi-stage scratch space
- References: `vec_reduce_max/vec_reduce_max.cu`, `vec_topk/vec_topk.cu`



## Other Useful APIs

| API | Description |
|---|---|
| `Dump(ddr_address, keep_format=false)` | Upload to DDR, optionally reformat on DDR (skipped in profile mode) |
| `Clear()` | Reset fragments and bindings while keeping manager instance |
| `Status()` | Print workspace/fragments/binding debug information |



## Result Enum Quick Reference

| Value | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `SUCCESS` | Success |
| `NO_ENOUGH_SPACE` | Insufficient space |
| `DRIVER_ERROR` | Driver error |
| `SIO_ERROR` | SRAM I/O error |
| `SRAM_NOT_FOUND` | No binding found for DDR address |
| `UNKNOWN_FORMAT` | Unknown format |
| `UPLOAD_ERROR` / `FREE_ERROR` | Upload/free failed |
| `SIZE_NOT_MATCH` | Size mismatch |
| `TENSOR_NOT_FOUND` | Tensor binding not found |



## Typical Managed SRAM Flow

```text
1. GetInstance()
2. Init(SRAM_WORKSPACE)
3. PitchAllocate / Allocate (bind each DDR buffer to SRAM)
4. Download (DDR -> SRAM)
5. kernel(GetSRamAddr(...))
6. Upload (SRAM -> DDR)
7. Destroy()
```



## When to Use `rpp_smgr.h` vs Manual Path

Prefer `rpp_smgr.h` when you need:

- managed DDR<->SRAM mapping
- tensor helpers
- format tracking and automatic reformat path
- shorter and cleaner code

Prefer manual APIs (`rtMallocSram` / `rtMemcpyDeviceToSram`) when you need:

- full manual control
- complex ping-pong SRAM scheduling
- bandwidth micro-benchmarks
- scratch buffers not bound to DDR
- low-level pipeline orchestration



## Summary

- Allocation: `PitchAllocate` / `Allocate` / `AllocateTensor*`
- Transfer: `Download` / `Upload` (+ 2D and Tensor variants)
- Address: `GetSRamAddr` for kernel access
- Format: `ReformatInPlace` / `ReformatTo` / `ReformatDDR`
- Lifecycle: usually initialize once with `Init`, explicitly call `Destroy`; use `Clear` for multi-case reuse
